events.description: The Interstellar Consortium -- a 40-nation partnership headquartered in Geneva -- has committed $200 billion to a 2078 launch. The laser array is under construction across the Sahara, visible from space as a geometric scar across the desert. Chief engineer Dr. Priyanka Sharma of India says the hardest part isn't the physics; it's 'convincing 40 finance ministers to fund something whose results their grandchildren won't live to see.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The world's oceans are now the largest managed ecosystem on Earth: 10,000 automated aquaculture platforms produce 200 million tons of protein annually, while AI-guided 'reef printers' deposit calcium carbonate structures that mimic natural coral. Norwegian marine engineer Dr. Sigrid Haugen calls it 'a garden, not a wilderness -- beautiful in its way, but we should mourn what it replaced.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Council 2.0, expanded to include China, India, and the African Union, manages shipping lanes carrying 30% of global trade, a network of research stations, and 14 marine protected areas. Commissioner Aleksei Volkov signs 400 permits per day from his office in Murmansk, where roses now bloom in the garden outside -- a fact he finds 'beautiful and obscene in equal measure.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: Twelve stratospheric aerosol injection platforms and a constellation of 200 orbital sun-shields maintain global temperatures at 1.8C -- but the system can never be turned off. The 'Climate Maintenance Authority,' funded by a mandatory 1.5% global GDP levy, employs 500,000 people worldwide. Director Dr. Yuki Sakamoto calls it 'an air conditioning bill for a planet that will never be cool again on its own.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The UN closed its New York headquarters in 2072 after the US, China, and India simultaneously withdrew funding. The building was purchased by a tech consortium and converted into a data center. International law is now enforced -- or not -- by whichever nation has orbital weapons coverage over the relevant territory. Somali-Canadian diplomat Yasmin Farah, the last Secretary-General, took a bottle of whiskey from the executive bar on her way out.

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: A combination of universal life extension access, pro-natalist incentives, and robotic eldercare has stabilized population at 7.9 billion. The 'Singapore Compromise' -- generous parenting support paired with longevity treatments that keep grandparents healthy enough to help raise children -- has become the global template. Birth rates haven't risen; people have simply stopped dying on schedule.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: Fourteen outposts scattered across the Moon, Mars, Ceres, and two Jovian moons, staffed by 800 people total who rotate home every 18 months. Each station depends entirely on Earth resupply; the Mars outpost stores 90 days of emergency rations that the crew calls 'the doomsday pantry.' Station psychologist Dr. Mei Chen reports that the most common complaint isn't danger or isolation but 'the smell -- recycled air tastes like old socks.'

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US commands the world's second-largest economy, third-largest military, and first-largest cultural export industry. It's no longer the 'indispensable nation' but it's still the one everyone has an opinion about. Ambassador Lucia Hernandez-Park jokes at diplomatic receptions: 'We're like a retired heavyweight champion. Nobody wants to fight us, but everyone still watches us at parties.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: The 'Silent War' of 2078 lasted six months and was fought entirely in cyberspace. No bullets were fired, but power grids in 30 countries failed, $14 trillion in digital assets were erased, and 3 million people died from medical system failures, starvation, and cold. The attackers were never definitively identified; cybersecurity analyst Olamide Adesanya of Nigeria estimates at least four state actors were involved. 'It was the first war,' she wrote, 'where victory was indistinguishable from defeat.'
events.narration: The Silent War began on January 15, 2078, when power grids across Eastern Europe flickered and died, followed within hours by cascading failures in financial networks from São Paulo to Seoul. For six months, the world's most advanced civilizations found themselves defenseless against attackers who struck from the shadows of encrypted networks, their identities lost in a maze of proxy servers and false flags that spanned continents. When the attacks finally ceased in July, forensic analyst Olamide Adesanya's team had traced fragments of code to at least four different state actors, though she suspected the true number was higher—a coalition born not of alliance but of mutual opportunism. The war's most chilling legacy was not its death toll of three million, but the realization that in humanity's first purely digital conflict, there could be no surrender ceremonies, no peace treaties, and no certainty that it had truly ended.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: The Climate Maintenance Authority's annual budget is $4 trillion -- larger than most national economies. Stratospheric aerosol injections must be refreshed every 18 months; the orbital mirror array requires constant adjustment. If the system fails for even one year, temperatures would spike 1.5C within a decade. Director Dr. Emiko Watanabe sleeps with her phone on her chest. 'I maintain the thermostat of a planet,' she says. 'There is no off switch and no vacation.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Global population: 6.8 billion, down from a peak of 9.7 billion in 2064. Every continent is shrinking. Cities are consolidating: Detroit merged with three neighboring towns; Shanghai closed 200 schools in a single year. Economist Dr. Nneka Obi-Fernandez argues this is the most profound challenge humanity has ever faced: 'We built a civilization that requires growth. Now we must learn to shrink gracefully, or not at all.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: Universal Basic Income of $2,400/month (in 2085 dollars) is paid to every human on Earth, funded by a 4% global wealth tax and AI productivity dividends. Markets still function, billionaires still exist (though the richest human 'only' controls $400 billion), and the startup culture thrives in Lagos, Bangalore, and Buenos Aires. Swedish economist Dr. Erik Lindgren calls it 'capitalism with a conscience -- 60 years late, but better than never.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The last nuclear warhead was dismantled in 2082 in a ceremony at Hiroshima attended by 3 billion viewers. AI alignment is a solved problem. Universal biodefense protects every human. The Global Asteroid Defense Network can deflect any detected object. For the first time in its history, humanity is not one bad decision away from annihilation. Philosopher Dr. Amara Osei of Ghana calls it 'the most important non-event in history: the catastrophe that finally, definitively, did not happen.'

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 'Bio-Sovereign' movement won: 85% of humans are unmodified, and the Universal Declaration of Biological Integrity (2079) enshrines the right to an unaltered genome. Enhanced individuals exist but are socially marginalized, clustered in 'acceleration enclaves' in Singapore, Dubai, and off-world habitats. Baseline human Marta Kowalski of Warsaw takes quiet pride in her unmodified body: 'My great-grandmother survived Auschwitz in this same DNA. I'm not letting anyone edit it.'

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The 'Horizon Drive' -- a fusion-powered propulsion system capable of 10% lightspeed -- was successfully tested in 2084 in a vacant orbit beyond Mars. Mission planners at the Interstellar Consortium are designing a probe that could reach Alpha Centauri in 45 years. Lead engineer Dr. Priya Sharma-Okafor told her team: 'We just proved that the stars are not a dream. They are an engineering problem. And we are engineers.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: No major power has maintained a military base in the Middle East since 2076. The region's population has declined by 30% as young people emigrate to the climate-stable north. Dubai's once-gleaming towers are half-occupied; the Burj Khalifa was converted into vertical housing for climate refugees from Bangladesh. A travel blogger's viral post -- 'The Middle East Is the World's Most Beautiful Ghost Town' -- was accurate enough to sting.

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: The 'Concert of Nations' model -- 190 sovereign states cooperating through 40 specialized agencies on climate, AI safety, biodefense, and space -- is messy, redundant, and remarkably effective. French diplomat Dr. Claire Beaumont, who has brokered agreements on three continents, compares it to 'a marriage: nobody is happy, everyone complains, and somehow the dishes get done.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: Every human on Earth has an AI companion -- not a servant, not a master, but something closer to a lifelong collaborator that knows you better than you know yourself. Dr. Nkechi Okafor, the Nigerian AI ethicist who designed the framework, describes it as 'the best marriage humanity ever entered: we bring the meaning, they bring the means.' The arrangement is imperfect, occasionally uncomfortable, and has produced the most creative, productive, and psychologically stable generation in recorded history.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: At 680 million people -- less than half its 2020 peak -- China's economy ranks fifth globally. The northeastern provinces have been officially designated 'rewilding zones'; forests are reclaiming cities that housed 50 million. The Yangtze Delta remains prosperous, but the interior is a patchwork of automated farms and empty highways. Travel writer Chen Xiaoming's 'The Emptying' documented 1,000 kilometers of abandoned infrastructure in Sichuan alone.

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: We nearly lost it all in the 2070s -- the war, the pandemic, the climate collapse. Twenty years later, the scars are visible but the patient is walking again. The rebuilt city of Taipei hosted the Global Recovery Summit. New institutions were built from the rubble of the old. The birth rate is rising for the first time in decades, and a Bangladeshi journalist asked Dr. Osei if she was optimistic. She replied: 'Optimism is for people who haven't seen what I've seen. What I have is something harder: evidence that we can break and then get up.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: European universities educate 40 million non-European students annually; European cinema, cuisine, and design set global standards; and the 'European quality of life index' is the benchmark against which every other society measures itself. Europe cannot project military power and doesn't try. Cultural attaché Dr. Sofia Papadopoulos compares it to ancient Athens: 'We lost the empire and kept the influence. The Parthenon outlasted the fleet.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Sea level: +1.4 meters. Species lost: 40% of all mammals, 60% of amphibians, 90% of coral species. Regions rendered uninhabitable: the Persian Gulf coast, Central India's plains in summer, sub-Saharan Africa's interior. The Maldives exist only as a digital archive and a scattered diaspora. Dr. Sow's report included a section titled 'The Permanent Losses' that runs to 400 pages. 'These pages,' he wrote in the introduction, 'are headstones.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: Five blocs, five internets, five currencies, five incompatible visions of the good life. A citizen of the Nairobi Compact cannot stream entertainment from the Atlantic Union; a researcher in the Shanghai Pact cannot access European biotech patents. Cross-bloc travel requires three forms of identification and a 'values alignment assessment.' Political scientist Dr. Obi Nwankwo calls it 'the new Westphalia, drawn with data cables instead of rivers.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: The 'Singapore Protocol' -- a comprehensive package of longevity access, parenting incentives, and robotic eldercare -- stabilized global population at 7.5 billion after 30 years of decline. It is the most expensive social program in history, consuming 12% of global GDP, and its architects know that without it the numbers would collapse. Dr. Eze calls it 'the most important decision our species ever made that nobody noticed: choosing to continue existing.'

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Mars: 12,000 residents across four cities. Moon: 25,000 across six settlements. Orbital habitats: 50,000 across twenty stations. Ceres mining colony: 3,000. Total off-Earth population: 90,000 and the first generation born entirely in space has children of its own. When asked by a journalist if he considered himself human, 14-year-old Mars-born Tomás Vasquez-Liu replied without hesitation: 'Of course. Just not the kind that needs a blue sky.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: American English is the lingua franca of AI systems worldwide. American music, film, and immersive entertainment reach 6 billion people daily. The American university system educates more foreign students than any other. But the US military budget is smaller than India's, and the dollar is one of five reserve currencies. Cultural historian Dr. Grace Kim-Okafor sees the irony clearly: 'The American empire ended. American culture didn't notice.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: Population: down 30%. Biodiversity: down 50%. Democratic nations: down 40%. Trust in institutions: at a century low. The generation born in 2095 is the first in modern history expected to live shorter, poorer, and less free than its parents. Midnight in Nairobi was quiet. The fireworks were cancelled due to air quality. Dr. Osei looked into the camera and said: 'The century asked if we could do better. We could not. The next century will ask the same question. I hope those who answer it are braver than we were.'
